---
title: "Golden Lab Data Science Workflows"
page-layout: full
toc: false
title-block-style: none
---

::: {.hero}
<div class="eyebrow">Golden Lab Data Science handbook[^1]</div>

# Data science workflows for research that lasts

This handbook aims to teach you everything you need to know about 
carrying out data science in the Golden Lab: working on the cluster, 
maintaining code as the source of truth, documenting decisions close to 
the analysis, and building projects that other people can reproduce.

[Start Here](01_orientation/start-here.qmd){.hero-button}
[See the Workflow Roadmap](01_orientation/workflow-roadmap.qmd){.hero-button .secondary}
:::

::: {.card-grid}
::: {.info-card}
### 🚀 Supercomputing, simplified

Treat HPC as the default, not the backup plan. Use FASRC's interactive 
apps for exploration and experimentation, and scale your analyses with SLURM jobs.

[Open the infrastructure guide](02_guides/hpc-infrastructure.qmd)
:::

::: {.info-card}
### 🏗️ Build projects that survive handoff

Make your science reproducible by using organized project structures, robust relative
paths, and fully specified software environments, to create research that runs _anywhere._

[Open the reproducibility guide](02_guides/projects-and-reproducibility.qmd)
:::

::: {.info-card}
### 📊 Handle data like shared lab infrastructure

Track data provenance, avoid duplication, document variables, and separate raw, 
analytic, and derived data.

[Open the data guide](02_guides/data-access-and-management.qmd)
:::

::: {.info-card}
### 🤝 Collaborate without chaos

Record your experiments with `git`, collaborate on code on GitHub, and 
document your work beautifully so others can support your work and build upon it.

[Open the collaboration guide](02_guides/collaboration-and-support.qmd)
:::
:::

## Lab Principles

::: {.principle-grid}
::: {.principle-card}
### 1. Remote & reproducible beats local & manual 🌐

If your work matters, it should run on any infrastructure, and be re-runnable from
code — not clicks.
:::

::: {.principle-card}
### 2. Code is the source of truth 💎

Results, figures, and tables are merely products of the workflow. The lasting 
record — and scientific truth underlying them — is in the code and 
documentation that created them.
:::

::: {.principle-card}
### 3. Analysis should be narrated 📇

Use notebooks and reports to explain what you did, why you did it, and what should happen next.
:::

::: {.principle-card}
### 4. Robust habits compound 🌱

Version control, clean project structure, and clear documentation 
reduces technical debt for your colleagues — and your future self.
:::
:::

## What students should be able to do

By the end of onboarding, a student should be able to:

1. Start a project on the correct FASRC system and choose an appropriate storage location.
2. Create a reproducible project structure with notebooks, scripts, and dependency tracking.
3. Read shared datasets without making unnecessary copies.
4. Push analysis code and documentation to GitHub without exposing restricted data.
5. Ask for help with enough context that someone else can reproduce the problem.

## Suggested reading path

1. Read [Start Here](01_orientation/start-here.qmd) for the workflow philosophy and first-week checklist.
2. Read [Workflow Roadmap](01_orientation/workflow-roadmap.qmd) for the full research lifecycle.
3. Use the guide pages as reference while you are actively working on a project.
4. Keep [Reference Library](resources.qmd) open when you need the official docs.

<!-- ## This website is itself part of the recommendation

R for Data Science frames communication as a core part of the data science process, and specifically points readers to Quarto for integrating prose, code, and results. That is the same pattern used here: documentation is not an afterthought, it is part of the workflow.[^r4ds-communicate] -->

[^1]: If you were looking for the Golden Lab Google Public Site, click [here(#TODO FIND THE OLD SITE)]().

<!-- [^r4ds-communicate]: Hadley Wickham, Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund, [*R for Data Science (2e): Communicate*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/communicate). -->
